nativeRange.habitat: Freshwater and occasionally slightly brackish lakes, ponds, slow-moving streams, and vegetated swamp/marsh margins. Commonly hidden among aquatic plants by day and emerging at night to feed.

decorPreferences: rock piles and shrimp caves for hiding
fine sand or small gravel substrate

diet.notes: Generally regarded as a scavenging omnivore documented eating biofilm, soft algae, decaying plant matter, detritus, and small aquatic invertebrates. Has been reported readily accepting sinking shrimp pellets, algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, cucumber), and bits of frozen foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp). Calcium- or mineral-supplemented foods are commonly recommended to support successful molting. Has been known to be somewhat more carnivorous and opportunistic than dwarf Neocaridina/Caridina shrimp, and may pick at very small or weakened tankmates.

goodWith: small peaceful community fish (small tetras, rasboras, danios)
other peaceful dwarf shrimp (in spacious tanks)
avoidWith: larger or predatory fish (cichlids, angelfish, larger gouramis)
any fish large enough to swallow an adult shrimp

imageCandidates.notes: Smithsonian Environmental Research Center, 1024x518. Tack-sharp full lateral of a live translucent individual clearly showing the near-transparent glass body, fine speckling and red-banded antennae/legs (diagnostic).

2592x1944. Live Ghost Shrimp in a planted, decorated home aquarium among driftwood and gravel; shows the transparent body in the actual hobbyist display context. Shrimp slightly soft, so kept as non-recommended.
640x480. Closer in-tank view of a single transparent individual, showing the glass-clear body detail; lower resolution, so non-recommended.

summary: The Ghost Shrimp is a small, nearly transparent North American freshwater shrimp widely sold as both a beginner display species and as feeder stock. It is generally considered easy to keep and is unusual among its genus in completing its full life cycle in pure freshwater.
careNotes: The trade name 'Ghost Shrimp' is applied loosely in the aquarium hobby and may cover several similar Palaemonetes species — including P. paludosus, P. kadiakensis, and P. pugio — as well as unrelated Macrobrachium juveniles and even dried marine ghost shrimp (family Callianassidae). P. paludosus is the most common form sold in pet stores as a live display or feeder animal, though species identity is best treated as approximate when shrimp come from bulk feeder tanks. Ghost Shrimp are generally among the more tolerant freshwater shrimp, accepting a wide range of pH and hardness values, but like all crustaceans they are sensitive to copper, ammonia, nitrite, and rapid shifts in water chemistry; they should only be added to a fully cycled aquarium, and copper-based medications must be avoided in any tank where they are kept. Ghost Shrimp purchased from feeder-stock tanks often arrive stressed and undernourished from high-density holding conditions, and a noticeable fraction may die within the first one to two weeks despite good care. Selecting healthy, active individuals and drip-acclimating them slowly on arrival is generally advised to improve survival. Ghost Shrimp tend to be somewhat jumpy, particularly when first introduced or startled, so a tightly fitting lid is commonly recommended.
breedingNotes: Unusually for the genus Palaemonetes, P. paludosus completes its full larval development in pure freshwater and does not require brackish or marine conditions for hatching, which is what makes it a viable freshwater-only aquarium breeder. Females reportedly carry between 8 and 85 eggs (averaging around 35) under the abdomen for approximately 12 to 14 days at 26-28°C. Hatched larvae pass through a planktonic stage lasting roughly 5 to 10 days before transforming into miniature adults; during this window they require very fine foods such as powdered spirulina, infusoria, freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, or green water, and are easily lost to filter intakes and predation by adults or fish. Raising fry in a dedicated, heavily planted, sponge-filtered tank with no fish predators is commonly reported to give the best survival rates. Adults will breed at standard community parameters, but elevated temperatures in the range of 26-28°C are reported to accelerate egg development.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op (Top 5 Freshwater Shrimp guide)
additional.notes: Substitute primary. Confirms temperature 21-27°C (70-80°F), pH above 7.0, higher GH preferred, mineral supplementation recommended, omnivorous/opportunistic diet, beginner care.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-confirms scientific name Palaemonetes paludosus, family Palaemonetes, North American native range, adult size ~3.8-5 cm, ~1 year lifespan, 18-28°C, pH 7.0-8.0, gH 3-12, 5-10 gallon tank, peaceful shy temperament, feeder-shrimp transport stress.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder
additional.notes: Cross-confirms adult size 3-5 cm, lifespan 1-3 years, 20-27°C, pH 6.5-8.0, gH 3-15, kH 3-12, 10-gallon minimum, freshwater larval development, brood size 8-85 eggs, 12-14 day incubation, 5-10 day planktonic larval phase, moderate escape risk, drip-acclimation recommendation.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Palaemonetes paludosus)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback: family Palaemonidae, order Decapoda. Confirms native range (southern US east of Appalachians, scattered records elsewhere), freshwater/slightly-brackish lake habitat, nocturnal feeding, up to 2.5 cm wild adult length, up to 85 eggs/clutch, keystone-prey ecological role.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts every 2-4 weeks depending on age, temperature, and feeding; juveniles molt more frequently than mature adults. Discarded exoskeletons are commonly left in the tank and re-consumed for calcium; they should not be removed. Insufficient mineral content (low gH, low calcium) is widely associated with incomplete-molt fatalities. Stable water parameters and adequate hiding places are generally considered the most important factors for successful molting, as freshly-molted shrimp are soft and vulnerable for several hours.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Unlike most Palaemonetes species, P. paludosus completes its larval cycle in pure freshwater, so no brackish or marine hatching tank is required. Breeding is still considered moderately challenging compared to Neocaridina shrimp, because the planktonic larval stage demands very fine foods such as infusoria, powdered spirulina, or brine shrimp nauplii, and the larvae need protection from filter intakes and adult cannibalism.
